I’ve always been both very smart & very kinesthetic. but there are clearer-laid societal paths for what to do with intellectual gifts than for what to do with sensory gifts

so I got a math degree first 🤷‍♀️ but touch was always there, too

text:
I hear people say “spiritual” to refer to two distinct categories of thing:

1. orienting to what my unique human role is amidst the larger forces of the world, and how to serve it well

2. connecting to intuitive / nonmaterial sense faculties (ie energetic, the somatic-imaginal)

even are.

so, lots of sensemaking about “how the world works” goes in category 1

they're going for "low-efficiency, high-tech" whose technical workings are inscrutable to the average user

I hadn't thought of those frames before, but they made immediate sense!

and gave me an inkling vision of tech that's patterned more in the shape of a balanced ecosystem.

which could look quite different than most of what we see now and take for granted as "what tech's like"

text:
she recommended me these two blogs which I look forward to perusing:

"There's Hundred Rabbits; they live on a sailboat while doing "experiments on resilience and self-reliance through low-tech solutions." They rederived their entire tech stack from scratch when they found most of their original stack wouldn't work when they sailed too far from the US. https://t.co/IKXmzKBwQn

I also like LOW<-TECH MAGAZINE (Kris De Decker), which is a refreshing independent publication about truly sustainable & efficient technology that considers not just pieces of technology in isolation, but also their systemic effects!

There’s an entirely solar-powered version of the website which had an uptime of 95.26% during 2019, but 3% of the downtime can be directly attributed to a software upgrade that increased the size of the Linux kernel by a whopping 25%.

One lovely mindful thing about the solar-powered site is that it always displays a battery indicator. To avoid wasting power (and bonus: my computer power) with my extremely inevitable bingefest, I bought their ebook compilations and insatiably binged the entirety of the 2012-2018 book like the book monster I am. A few favorite articles from said binge:

• How and why I stopped buying new laptops: on the author’s everyday usage of exclusively older laptops
•
• Fruit Walls & Reinventing The Greenhouse: a fascinating two-part micro-history on the development of greenhouses, and their 17th century precursor, solar-absorbing thermal mass
•
• In general, most of the Obsolete Technology section, which researches largely forgotten technologies that have been reinvented for modern use or may still be relevant today
•
• How To Build A Low-Tech Internet: important lessons from internet infrastructure in developing countries
•
• The STANGENKUNSTEN, Jerker Line Systems, and Endless Rope Drives. An absolutely delightful three-part microhistory into multi-kilometer power transmission through assemblages of linked rods and ropes, and how those might still be relevant to us today
•
• Well-Tended Fires Outperform Cooking Stoves. Hear them out.
A lot of LOW<-TECH’s efficiency analyses center the energy inefficiencies incurred by intermediate layers of energy translation.
For example, modern electric appliances running off grids incur absurd translation-layer losses:
mine the coal/fossil-fuels —> burn them —> convert that to electricity —> transfer a long way across a grid —> finally, convert it back to heat
Good food for thought about the entire lifecycle of the energy you use, not just how efficient it is after it comes out of your outlet.

They rederived their entire tech stack from scratch when they found most of their original stack wouldn't work when they sailed too far from the US. https://t.co/IKXmzKBwQn

I also like LOW<-TECH MAGAZINE (Kris De Decker), which is a refreshing independent publication about truly sustainable & efficient technology that considers not just pieces of technology in isolation, but also their systemic effects!

There’s an entirely solar-powered version of the website which had an uptime of 95.26% during 2019, but 3% of the downtime can be directly attributed to a software upgrade that increased the size of the Linux kernel by a whopping 25%.

One lovely mindful thing about the solar-powered site is that it always displays a battery indicator. To avoid wasting power (and bonus: my computer power) with my extremely inevitable bingefest, I bought their ebook compilations and insatiably binged the entirety of the 2012-2018 book like the book monster I am. A few favorite articles from said binge:

• How and why I stopped buying new laptops: on the author’s everyday usage of exclusively older laptops
•
• Fruit Walls & Reinventing The Greenhouse: a fascinating two-part micro-history on the development of greenhouses, and their 17th century precursor, solar-absorbing thermal mass
•
• In general, most of the Obsolete Technology section, which researches largely forgotten technologies that have been reinvented for modern use or may still be relevant today
•
• How To Build A Low-Tech Internet: important lessons from internet infrastructure in developing countries
•
• The STANGENKUNSTEN, Jerker Line Systems, and Endless Rope Drives. An absolutely delightful three-part microhistory into multi-kilometer power transmission through assemblages of linked rods and ropes, and how those might still be relevant to us today
•
• Well-Tended Fires Outperform Cooking Stoves. Hear them out.
A lot of LOW<-TECH’s efficiency analyses center the energy inefficiencies incurred by intermediate layers of energy translation.
For example, modern electric appliances running off grids incur absurd translation-layer losses:
mine the coal/fossil-fuels —> burn them —> convert that to electricity —> transfer a long way across a grid —> finally, convert it back to heat
Good food for thought about the entire lifecycle of the energy you use, not just how efficient it is after it comes out of your outlet.

text:
it's weirdly hard to find a straight answer to "what's good posture"

my understanding now is:
* evenly-distributed muscle tone. no part is overtired or doing comparatively way more than others
* smooth flow of kinetic energy through the body. no 'catches' or places it gets stuck

* habit

habit: structure can do it but, some other position "feels comfier"

text: In the unknown spaces, strange metamorphoses begin. Buried neck deep in the cool earth of the Kalahari at night, the patient heals. In the dark Greek cave, the sacred dogs of Asclepius lick the patient's skin clean.

text: Here, bones reknit themselves, and tissues regrow. At what pace does a cell wall repair? At what pace does a wound scab over? At what pace do Achilles tendons heal? Trust me, not, at our desired pace.

text: How many times have we gotten an injury and wanted to be further along than we actually were? Yet we had to adjust. And in that adjustment - a window opened to something vast.

text: For Apollo, it is to be remembered - Apollo, the healer. Apollo appeared most often to those who incubated in caves.

raw.full_text: For Apollo, it is to be remembered - Apollo, the healer. Apollo appeared most often to those who incubated in caves.

text: And perhaps so it is with our world. If we want to help heal the world, then we need to understand and start to align ourselves to the pace at which worlds actually heal.

raw.full_text: And perhaps so it is with our world. If we want to help heal the world, then we need to understand and start to align ourselves to the pace at which worlds actually heal.

text:
So healing happens not through talking about healing, or theorizing our way towards healing, but through the actual somatic process of bone growth and cell renewal over time. 

Not according to our desired timescale - according to nature's time scale.

raw.full_text:
So healing happens not through talking about healing, or theorizing our way towards healing, but through the actual somatic process of bone growth and cell renewal over time. 

Not according to our desired timescale - according to nature's time scale.

text:
This means, for example, when we hear people say, "oh, all those problems were over 100years ago, shouldn't we be over that by now?" 

That is not a reflection of the actual pace at which things change, at which things work their way through muscles & blood & bone & lineage.

raw.full_text:
This means, for example, when we hear people say, "oh, all those problems were over 100years ago, shouldn't we be over that by now?"

text: And when scientists say things like “we've only got three years to address climate change,” it might be a good wake up call or alarm bell, but what does it actually say? What planetary systems heal in three years?

raw.full_text: And when scientists say things like “we've only got three years to address climate change,” it might be a good wake up call or alarm bell, but what does it actually say? What planetary systems heal in three years?

text: Yes, we should put our effort into stopping carbon emissions, we should do everything we can. But it also may very well be that planetary healing is something that happens on a timescale far beyond us, in spite of us, and utterly out of our control.

raw.full_text: Yes, we should put our effort into stopping carbon emissions, we should do everything we can. But it also may very well be that planetary healing is something that happens on a timescale far beyond us, in spite of us, and utterly out of our control.

text: Like Trevor Hall says in the song, “you can't rush your healing.” But boy, do we try to rush it.

raw.full_text: Like Trevor Hall says in the song, “you can't rush your healing.” But boy, do we try to rush it.

text: Because anxiousness, right? Anxiousness fears nothing more than unknown space. Unknown space, the yawning cave, the deep underground aquifer, the void of space, time that operates in time scales that have nothing to do with us - is terrifying.

raw.full_text: Because anxiousness, right? Anxiousness fears nothing more than unknown space. Unknown space, the yawning cave, the deep underground aquifer, the void of space, time that operates in time scales that have nothing to do with us - is terrifying.

text: And to enter that primordial space is to encounter, at first, an almost unfathomable grief at the state of things, grief at the state of things and what we've done to this world and how we ended up here.

raw.full_text: And to enter that primordial space is to encounter, at first, an almost unfathomable grief at the state of things, grief at the state of things and what we've done to this world and how we ended up here.

text: And then a reactive wave comes that wants to immediately agitate us, right out of there. I can't possibly go in there, into the deep, quiet dark. If I were to let go of this pattern of agitation, this encompassing architecture of control, who would I be?

raw.full_text: And then a reactive wave comes that wants to immediately agitate us, right out of there. I can't possibly go in there, into the deep, quiet dark. If I were to let go of this pattern of agitation, this encompassing architecture of control, who would I be?

text:
If I were to unplug for three days, who would I be? If I were to let go of that plan that I already know isn't going to happen but I've staked my identity on, what would my life be then?

What if I'm not totally on schedule for all that I want to do and achieve?

text:
But for those who weather that first wave and step across the threshold, what do we find?

The Void. The awe-some recognition that we too will be swallowed by time, all of our actions in this life, ultimately for what?

text: And you know this place. “I might not leave a legacy. I might not save the world.” Despite all my best efforts, I might not save the world.

text:
In fact, I might have no control over what happens at all. In the great movement of time and space and shifts in the earth-tectonics and atmosphere, and waves of species dominance and extinction - I might be nothing. I might be nothing.

And there, there - is the gateway.

text: Removing all our ornaments, we slip to the healing vastness - absorbed into the hum of forever. A forever into which we die, and are born, and die and are born, breath by breath.

text: And from that place - that slow, deep place - we breathe new breath. And new songs arrive. And we open our eyes to a world that is present, that is here, that is now - alive, singing, wanting nothing but our careful attention.

text:
but nevertheless. we go looking for pleats in a partner’s knee joint capsules. we start with the knee because it is the biggest joint capsule, an average of 5/8 inch away from the joint line! 

I find - “something.” a bump. teacher comes over and confirms it’s a pleat. precisely describes the exact folds and directions of each. I’m like???? ok cool maybe I’ll feel that in years. but right now I simply have my draw to the area, and a general feeling of a sticky bump 

and I invoke regenerativity, tuning into deep relaxation in myself, and connecting with the tissue about it. nice, it starts moving, uh this is totally not the unfold-the-pleats technique we were taught BUT the tissue’s unwinding and my partner’s breathing deepens 

I call the teacher back over - “so, something happened, but I’m not sure if it got the pleat, can you check?”

he checks, and laughs “pleat? what pleat? yeah this resolved, good work” 

🙌🙌🙌

raw.full_text:
the core instructions for a regenerative touch practitioner: 

1. there is a particular somatic state I occupy as a practitioner. I call it Kairos. connection to primordial deep time. then I tuning-fork it out, too, so their body can pick up on the dream trance

text: 1.5 it’s important that I stay in this state!!! and, helpfully, this state is supported by certain kinds of movement and touch, probably ones that move at the speed of fascia. do those movements and those qualities of touch.

raw.full_text: 1.5 it’s important that I stay in this state!!! and, helpfully, this state is supported by certain kinds of movement and touch, probably ones that move at the speed of fascia. do those movements and those qualities of touch.

text: 2. be a “follow” for their body unwinding. (If this is a partner dance, they are actually the lead)

raw.full_text: 2. be a “follow” for their body unwinding. (If this is a partner dance, they are actually the lead)

text:
IN THE BACKGROUND - keep honing tactile sensitivity, at every opportunity!! all these steps get more potent the more I’m able to feel. 

but, “honing tactile sensitivity” is a slow background process that makes a huge difference between session 1 and session 100.

like one of those jello fruit cakes https://t.co/xopDhezltJ

text:
this is how my work is so generalizable - I can work with conditions even if I don't know the names of the structures involved, because I'm familiar with what the gel wants to feel like

and can detect deviations from that (often known as 'fibrosities' - adhesions & contractures)

text:
Even if you're interested in esoteric bodywork, I recommend "non-agenda'd relaxing massage" as a starting skill! 

My modality, regenerative touch, is so highly dependent on being able to feel responsiveness in the tissues. But not everyone has that immediately, and relaxation massage is still high-benefit for the receiver from a total beginner. Relaxation massage lets you observe someone else's body without an agenda, so that over time you refine the skill of haptically seeing. 

This is in contrast to starting with an agenda'd way of seeing the body - for example, trigger point work. I suspect an inexperience session with someone who's trying to shift your body equilibrium has a higher chance of being really unpleasant, versus a relaxation massage by someone inexperienced is often still quite good.

text:
I see people using the word “belief” to two distinctly different things, both very real:

* beliefs that make testable claims (“I believe there is a rock behind that tree”)

* beliefs that organize consciousness (“the earth wants me to live well,” “I am loveable” etc)

I see people using the word “belief” to two distinctly different things, both very real:

* beliefs that make testable claims (“I believe there is a rock behind that tree”)

* beliefs that organize consciousness (“the earth wants me to live well,” “I am loveable” etc)

I was in one of Myles’s classes and they did a similar “ok class, what is everyone perceiving in this body” exercise with a volunteer

the class said like 15 different things (“why is his forehead so tight”) and I perceived ~2 of them. my biggest ping was something no one said

text: meanwhile Myles nodded at every single one of the 15 things 😂 and maintained connection with each person who spoke. so a lot of them got to trust “wow, there really is a strong signal in subjective feeling-space”

raw.full_text: meanwhile Myles nodded at every single one of the 15 things 😂 and maintained connection with each person who spoke. so a lot of them got to trust “wow, there really is a strong signal in subjective feeling-space”

text:
also, pregnancy, birth, and infancy are such physical processes. 

imo this is where people who’re otherwise happy to live disembodied-ly 

will most likely meet the health benefits of embodiment. when a new baby’s life literally depends on family ability to read physical cues

raw.full_text:
also, pregnancy, birth, and infancy are such physical processes. 

imo this is where people who’re otherwise happy to live disembodied-ly 

will most likely meet the health benefits of embodiment. when a new baby’s life literally depends on family ability to read physical cues

text:
the days include stories like - here’s an excerpt from my notes - 

“There’s this fussy time in the evening where babies are on and off the breast, on and off the breast - and sometimes parents will get exasperated and just give them a supplemental bottle of milk they pumped earlier in the morning. But really this is not a good thing to do because the milk at the end of the supply, after baby’s drained most of the breast, is higher-fat. It’s like the “cream.” And it sets a baby up to sleep longer through the night. Morning milk is a different composition that wakes them up more. So if you do need to top the baby off, the best way would be milk that you’d somehow pumped after they fell asleep. 

And this is not even to mention the different hormone levels through the day - for example, morning milk has more cortisol.”

(Now a lactation consultant in the room says there’s been a shift to “pooling milk” for the day, to smooth out variation)

methods that require me to be there. because I am there.

whatever I spec out is definitionally what someone else could also do

text:
I guess there could be two different ways to look at efficiency, above:

1. “make my own sessions the most potent they can be” aka lean hardest into my own particular nonspecificiable aliveness
2. generally spread touch skills to more folks. this comes at risk of losing aliveness

text:
sometimes I work with people outside my usual client pool - shorter sessions in groups of people etc. 

here’s quick oneliners of my assessments of the last 10 of those sessions went. it’s cool to see the sheer variety in bodies

* these fascia don’t even seem to realize they can communicate with one another. but at least they’re all the same texture. albeit it’s probably too pliable of a texture. also wow that limb is off in space  

* in good shape, I was doing minor things

* could use a few massages

* in good shape but got a lot out of me doing minor things; liked feeling energetically seen 

* really needs a lot of massage and regeneration 

* could really use a lot of massage and regeneration 

* emotional journey; heart connection 

* we straightforwardly made a lot of progress on a particular injury while he relaxed 

* in great shape but with one niche quite difficult problem 

* youthful resilient and coherent. could use one massage but, otherwise I think he’s fine and should only see me if he feels like it (which he’s welcome to!)

If I had to pick the #1 highest-impact but least-known idea in bodywork -

it’s that the body has an order of operations, and this order is discernible via tissue texture changes. 

just because something is stuck does not mean release it. find what’s already trying to unwind

text:
one of my most surprising takeaways from scar class

is that for all my fancy bodywork knowledge - there’s a technique called “dropping” where I simply drop my hand onto their body, from 1-2 inches up. and repeat.

works so well. the complexity is in the listening not the doing

raw.full_text:
one of my most surprising takeaways from scar class

is that for all my fancy bodywork knowledge - there’s a technique called “dropping” where I simply drop my hand onto their body, from 1-2 inches up. and repeat.

works so well. the complexity is in the listening not the doing

text:
it specifically works well on largeee uninterrupted regions of fascial net. so, abdomens. or I used it on a client with breast scarring and she found it deeply regulating.

each time I do it I laugh because it is so simple.

raw.full_text:
it specifically works well on largeee uninterrupted regions of fascial net. so, abdomens. or I used it on a client with breast scarring and she found it deeply regulating.

each time I do it I laugh because it is so simple.

text:
but there is a skilled part! which is that; 

after each drop, I sit and haptically listen for the reverberation effect through the fascial net. it feels that they (electrically?) wake up, and pulse & soften some. when that settles down I drop again, and wait again. & repeat

text: "By nature, some days are going to be better than others, and not every day is going to be the best day.  Recognizing that there's a spectrum of possible performances I can have in a day, and because it's a spectrum, sometimes I will be on the lower end of that spectrum.

raw.full_text: "By nature, some days are going to be better than others, and not every day is going to be the best day.  Recognizing that there's a spectrum of possible performances I can have in a day, and because it's a spectrum, sometimes I will be on the lower end of that spectrum.

text: And just - having to work on a long enough timespan that you develop a resilience to bad days - recognize that they will come, and they will go, and sometimes you'll just have a dud - that's good. That's a good lesson to learn.

raw.full_text: And just - having to work on a long enough timespan that you develop a resilience to bad days - recognize that they will come, and they will go, and sometimes you'll just have a dud - that's good. That's a good lesson to learn.

text: I think I'm way better at handling a dud now without feeling like I've personally failed."

text:
### On Giving Beauty Space

There's a friend I feel a strong artistic connection with. But a friction point in the dynamic, too.

Where he all this beautiful music, but he never quite clears enough space for each song to settle into, to be felt, and to move us.

It's a relentless machine-drum lurch forward from one song to the next.

He makes one playlist. I listen, and I feel a rising tide of starstruck, the songs infuse into my experience and surroundings,

and then before the tide has receded or even crested

before it has finished working me over

another playlist. And I feel cut off.

"Wait, weren't we doing something here? Could these flickering notes that I first heard 6 years ago in Berlin, that shaped me then, and you've somehow called up for us dancing now -

Could this not have a chance to be a lasting connection between us?"

Nope. Next playlist. Next dance event. It becomes a blur, and I wonder if I even felt anything.

So I feel a little uneasy now, around spotify playlists that are hours long, where each vibematched song hits a stylistically-consistent note of beauty that I've never heard before, and yet they are quickly passed over for the next song.

Sometimes I pick one song and I listen to it all day. Back when I had a radio show, making a one-hour playlist every two weeks felt a little fast.

Now that there's infinite music we could play anytime, I realize I like to let it seep. To let it pour from my memory and flavor the life around me.

text:
on re-listen I'm really happy with how meaning-dense all the lyrics are.  

> he falls asleep so quickly 
> but he never quite feels rested

anyone who knows this guy knows he is borderline narcoleptic. truly, he falls asleep within 30 seconds of climbing into bed. sometimes even before bed.

but then also, we never did have the "our bodies sleep so well together, we instantly co-relax" peaceful connection that some couples talk about. his body never quite felt at peace or settled. always abuzz, skewing agitated or manic, under a lid of humor. once he asked me what I bodywork-read in his body and I answered honestly that the vibe was "wheeee I'm FINE!!! hahaaaa don't help me" 

and yet. a clear sweetness for me in co-sleeping for three months, even so. feels like the sweetness bridged well over the foundational instability, while it did.

> but he never quite feels rested

anyone who knows this guy knows he is borderline narcoleptic. truly, he falls asleep within 30 seconds of climbing into bed. sometimes even before bed.

but then also, we never did have the "our bodies sleep so well together, we instantly co-relax" peaceful connection that some couples talk about. his body never quite felt at peace or settled. always abuzz, skewing agitated or manic, under a lid of humor. once he asked me what I bodywork-read in his body and I answered honestly that the vibe was "wheeee I'm FINE!!! hahaaaa don't help me" 

and yet. a clear sweetness for me in co-sleeping for three months, even so. feels like the sweetness bridged well over the foundational instability, while it did.

text:
Even though it didn't last, I'm grateful for the chance to really enter the space of my romantic love. To walk around in it, to learn what shape it is. To learn its edges, and how they overlap with another. Where these convergences can spring flows of deep fulfillment and sustenance, and where they can feel frictions.

I have a sense that lasting romance is when two such shapes "meet," and the flows through their overlaps make an overall upwards-spiral. But, they have to be compatible shapes for that...!

text:
here are my notes-to-self from watching

> a theory of what's going wrong in the modern world and what we can do about it

* overfit is about time horizons. it looks like you're doing amazing on everything you've already seen, but the problem is it flubs the never-seen-before next thing

* "ML models penalize complexity, but in our lives we don't, we rely on or own sense of overwhelm. we don't quantify the complexity cost of adding an extra messaging channel"

* modernity is the systematization of 'accuracy' but we forgot to ask - at what time horizons?

* "I think Seeing Like A State actually understates the problem. models are always missing things. it's not a question of "let's model *better*""

* expected risk and ambiguity. the world is always changing. models always go stale. we live in a world of ambuigity. the world is non-stationary. a model that is perfectly well-fit today is necessarily overfit over time. you have to leave slack in the system - if you don't, you're definitionally over fit.

* in modernity,  we are fully systematic about accuracy, and we're kind of loosey-goosey about the whole slack and complexity thing because we can't measure it

* the optimal amount to value "updating to the universe" versus "changing yourself" is equally. however many bits you want to diverge from what you were previously doing, you better produce more than that many bits of new accuracy. whatever you did to get here, worked! even if you don't know why.

* business cycle used to be 4 years boom one year bust - that was its accuracy/complexity cycle. now it's about 15 years boom and that's a sign of overfitting. the numbers - everything we know how to measure - are going up and to the right but something feels off.

* connection used to be scarce. now we're really connected, and sparsity is scarce. the ideal is somewhere in the middle

* what would it look like to manage complexity at a systemic level?
* on a personal level: stop consuming news sources that are high-frequency. they are high-complexity and overfitting. talk to your friends. be local.

* "global value is not worth as much as local value" - so we are overpaying the producers of global value.

* the modest proposal is an L2 loss over our society's information channels

text:
my THEORY for why this happened is that the right hip restriction was actually compensation to protect restriction around my iliocecal valve - the transition from small to large intestine. so straining my right hip might have opened the iliocecal valve and temporarily given me SIBO.

I’d been hungry before the class but, right after my appetite tanked and I felt inflammation in my lower right quadrant of my abdomen.

then I self-bodyworked the lower right abdominal quadrant, and it started responding and gurgling a lot, but not fully resolved, but better. then I allowed my right hip to be as crooked as it wanted and that also took the inflammation down by 20%, and it settled over the rest of the day.

caveats on this theory: I cannot yet feel the illogical valve, between large and small intestine. but I do know that’s the right spot for it, and I have a lot of pre-existing restriction there.

So my general theory is that my right hip is rotated forward to protect that valve from being stuck open, and will not unrotate until restriction around the valve is resolved. order of operations in action.

I’m curious @DrMylesDC if you have seen right hip restriction interacting with SIBO symptoms before

text:
generally I think that "soul purpose" is the wholesome underlying core of the thing that the western desire to "live up to my potential" or "self-actualize" is actually aiming at.

but I like it more because, it's less co-optable by the desire that "more is better" - no, you have a purpose. you do it. it's the thing that arises and comes to you when you make appropriate space for it. the work is in the balance of listening and implementing; you don't get extra points for overexerting or doing "more"

text:
woah I'm starting to be able to feel age of an injury + its developmental effects on the rest of the body structure!

fresh injuries (1 year) are very different than 20-year ago injuries, especially ones people got as a kid

my right ribs feel "duller" and less springy than my left ribs, and the right side was the one that got impacted in a car crash when I was in second grade. I wonder if the stored impact from the accident affected the tissue growth signaling somehow (or if the 'dullness' was perhaps purely due to being tugged on by surrounding compacted tissues on the right side, too)

text:
ayurveda x bodywork musings:

I actually underlyingly suspect that doshas are intended to be "means of perception" in the same way that my fasciavision is. and, attempts to filter dosha-understanding through analytical sensemaking are misguided.

this would also explain why I am not very good at perceiving doshas right now. my perceptual capacities are a coherent system, and I tend to actually reject imaginal structures that take more underlying complexity to boot up than they reward with additional ability to navigate the underlying bodily state space.

see the below post for more context. especially the part about
> what I feel is not “everything there is to feel” but rather a relatively small-but-complete basis set

so maybe all the lookup tables of ayurvedic properties etc, are just aiming to cultivate people's direct perceptual capacities into a particular ayurvedashape. if this is true, this excites me, becauuse a friend of mine is developing a gut-voice-message sense of people's doshas and prakritis simply by looking at them, and that seems like she's totally learning it :)

text:
I was talking to @radiant_body_ about my theory of why I don't like deep tissue massage super much - "I think since I have so much intestinal restriction, any attempt to do deep tissue on the right hip will just uncomfortably yank on that"

and she says "well if it's good deep tissue it shouldn't be yanking, it should be cathartically meeting the tissue and melting" 

and that struck me. she definitely has a point; I wondered if I'd been unnecessarily writing off the modality. but I do think I'm onto something where - with the level of intestinal adhesion I have currently on the surface, even *my own movement,* if not careful, can uncomfortably yank on the restricted clump :/ 

and that's why I'm in yoga class an hour a day for at least the next month or two. a year ago I had mega muscular compensations and restricted movement patterns all around this intestinal restriction, but now it's finally on the surface and accessible to the self-unwinding that I modify every yoga class to include. this is sure all taking a lot of attention to unwind, but I'd rather not turn around and go back - the compensation patterns were definitely a local maximum, not a global maximum. 

it also reminds me of a comment @DrMylesDC made in a bodywork class once. there's a common osteopathic technique called "slack"ing the tissues, and then bringing them up to "first barrier load." Myles was working on a classmate and said "wow this is way past first barrier, this is more like 15th barrier" and I think my right side intestines are currently like that. there are some pieces that are already at 20th barrier, and so they're amassing a lot of muscular compensation around them, to try to decrease the amount that they feel yanked or strained as I'm simply living my life. 

(ideally all tissues in resting state would be at 0-th barrier)

quoted: details of my abdominal restriction clump interacting with the rest of my body positioning

https://t.co/Pfhla99tTd

I once heard a question “why can’t our emotions be fully present-moment updated all the time?”

but to me that’s like asking “why do we both inhale and exhale? why can’t we just only ever inhale”

we take in, and also we release. it’s a cycle baked into the structure of nature

raw.full_text:
I once heard a question “why can’t our emotions be fully present-moment updated all the time?”

but to me that’s like asking “why do we both inhale and exhale? why can’t we just only ever inhale”

we take in, and also we release. it’s a cycle baked into the structure of nature

text:
and you can see in the inhale/exhale model that, clearly problems arise if you spend too much time in one half of the cycle.

just like with stuck emotions and unresolved experience.

but the update cycle itself is healthy; and imo the ‘release’ is connected to humility and death

and this is the convergence - if it's ready to go, it does feel effortless! like something you could have relaxed all along, like an 'effortless undoing'

but my divergence from the meditation and awareness practices is, I track a whole underlying loadbalanced model of tensions

text: in my model of body tension, the whole craft of my work is - assessing what tension is "ready to go" based on the shared capacity that the client and I have together in our systems at the time. just because it's stuck doesn't mean release it.

text:
that said, as I've recovered from chronic disease hell (!) and started hanging out with more Normal People who've never been Seriously Ill

a lot of them have neither of these two ingredients and still have baseline good gut health 🤷‍♀️ my working model is that they just got lucky.

meaning, they've never "depleted their stores of vital energy" - so, they've always had a buffer that self-replenishes faster than uninformed choices deplete it.

whereas chronic illness is the state that arises when various reserves have been depleted. so all the autobalancing of various flow rates is not-working-as-usual, and needs really great interoception (or a practitioner) to reach a healthy systemic equilibrium again.

where is luck involved in this? in the initial sort of "who depletes some sort of vital buffer." it's not something I was personally taught to guard against, so I kind of plowed right into it. and wow, it is such a project getting out of it. but if some people simply avoid it altogether, it can look to them like health is simple and straightforward. maintaining seems to be way easier than (certain kinds of) fixing.

a lot of them have neither of these two ingredients and still have baseline good gut health 🤷‍♀️ my working model is that they just got lucky.

meaning, they've never "depleted their stores of vital energy" - so, they've always had a buffer that self-replenishes faster than uninformed choices deplete it.

whereas chronic illness is the state that arises when various reserves have been depleted. so all the autobalancing of various flow rates is not-working-as-usual, and needs really great interoception (or a practitioner) to reach a healthy systemic equilibrium again.

where is luck involved in this? in the initial sort of "who depletes some sort of vital buffer." it's not something I was personally taught to guard against, so I kind of plowed right into it. and wow, it is such a project getting out of it. but if some people simply avoid it altogether, it can look to them like health is simple and straightforward. maintaining seems to be way easier than (certain kinds of) fixing.

text:
I asked @jessicavellela about ayurveda’s vision of “full vibrant health” - how do they define the goal state, svastha? what level of health challenge is “just part of life” versus what is an indication of an addressable underlying problem? 

some basics include: allergies, food sensitivities, energy issues etc are early warning signs of something that might compound. also full health includes emotions + ‘a sparkle in your eye’.  (something we don't get into: as a bodyworker, I know that ayurveda has long lists of textural qualities associated with “healthy tissue” that I am keen to learn, and we didn’t even mention.)

so “the set of challenges that are actually addressable” is definitely bigger than what western medical has learned to resolve. and Jess has done something very very hard, and studied diligently enough to learn to resolve a good chunk of them. I’ve got this scattered throughout lots of my tweets but now it’s on video too 😊

0:25 - svastha is very systematic! checks for "ranges of operation", though impressively does this all via sensory cues, not molecular measurements. 

1:30 - reflecting back to two years ago, when I wondered if some level of struggle / distress after eating was simply normal

3:15 - Q: what are some things Jess sees people assume are "just how life is" that are actually ayurvedic signs of imbalance? A: many over-the-counter medications (antihistamines, sleep aids, allergy meds) are not suppose to be happening in the first place. also we did not mention this, but, food sensitivities!

4:20 - letting in the enormity of: these signs and symptoms actually want something real from us, in terms of diet + lifestyle shifts. as far as I can tell, there isn't actually another option, it's either that or decline? 

6:10 - If you look at 10 randomly sampled people on the street, what does the "most svastha" of them look like?

text:
It’s been strange to update my self-concept from “I have no major injuries but more health and emotional struggles than most people seem to”

to

“oh woah that car crash I was in when I was 8 could’ve killed me, actually, and is still messing up my digestion 23 years later”

raw.full_text:
It’s been strange to update my self-concept from “I have no major injuries but more health and emotional struggles than most people seem to”

to

“oh woah that car crash I was in when I was 8 could’ve killed me, actually, and is still messing up my digestion 23 years later”

text:
it was a roll-over crash, the car flipped over, I was asleep in the back seat and must have, at some point, been yanked by a seatbelt around my waist (I was buckled in while lying down), maybe even fully suspended from the ceiling by it

honestly, grateful my back did not snap

text:
when I started learning bodywork I found that nothing in my right side abdomen was even remotely the texture it usually is on others.

working that out has been a yearslong process of - rehydrating the congealed layers, and settling out whatever inflammation got stuck in there.

raw.full_text:
when I started learning bodywork I found that nothing in my right side abdomen was even remotely the texture it usually is on others.

working that out has been a yearslong process of - rehydrating the congealed layers, and settling out whatever inflammation got stuck in there.

text:
even today there’s another layer; my ascending colon feels like somebody slammed it in a doorjamb and it’s bruised and sore.

but, it’s clearly clearly way better than it used to be, and this is a process of unwinding & cleaning the layers. or, unpacking the zip files.

raw.full_text:
even today there’s another layer; my ascending colon feels like somebody slammed it in a doorjamb and it’s bruised and sore.

text: and, it wasn’t really that “the crash was that bad” but that my whole body grew up with that injury in it, unbeknownst to it. and weak links in the chain tend to accumulate all the other metabolic dirt and grime, and be vulnerabilities for future injuries.

there is not enough existing accessible high-quality bodywork that I could’ve hired someone for

text:
talked to someone new to bodywork yesterday, wasn’t sure what would stick with someone totally fresh so I asked her for a reflection! here it is

- closing mental tabs and meditation in start 
- ⁠70/30 attention on your body vs theirs!
- ⁠hands can see 3-D!! I conceptualized it and then felt it on me
- ⁠year and a half journey to healing; these things take time, slowly by slowly
- ⁠didn’t know your journey but was pulling the generative thread
- ⁠gifting yourself a month of loving attention (and I love how you hold your heart self when u say this)
- ⁠having a sense of awe at the technical and the technique - and loved the connective tissues discussion!
- ⁠really connecting to your genuine expression of yourself. Loving how grounded you are in yourself ☺️

text:
“may all beings be happy” as a meditative mantra always confused me, because it seems straightforwardly ecologically impractical - life of some requires death of other. rabbits & wolves etc 

but a friend encouraged me to reframe it as “may the web of ecology and society and all its participants be in right relationship”

and I’m like. wow why didn’t you just say that directly! words mean things, I care about using them precisely.

text:
ok there is a specific technique I am internally calling the "regenerative stepback"

it is an awareness technique. sometimes I will get SO hyperfocused on particular textures and have a story of how they should go. but I'll be holding that story in a stuck / contracted way. 

then I remember humility and put my awareness in my spine and acknowledge that there's a whole wide world out there, and my client's body has its own processes and timeline of which I know nothing 

and with >70% hit rate, their tissue takes that moment to start spontaneously unwinding.

@DrMylesDC has mentioned something similar - about approaching a body with "search and destroy" attention versus spacious open awareness, ha

text:
here’s a sketch of three modules, two hours each:

1. Textural signals & literacy 
* Have you noticed some parts of bodies feel different than other parts? Or some bodies feel different than others? What’s that about (lab, people feel one another’s forearm and compare)
* Have you noticed some of these bodies and textures respond to your touch and sometimes no

2. Qualities of touch
* You are always touching from *some* quality of touch. But there are many, and honing / expanding this toolkit is the craft of bodywork! Here are a few starter qualities, that of course deepen over time as well. 

3. Regenerative touch philosophy
* In the beginning there was sensation
* Primordial slow time / “fallow” + mythosomatic listening + self-organizational qualities of the body 
* Reality has a surprising amount of detail

text:
teaching another class soon, yay! with @DrMylesDC :)

Regenerative Touch: An Experiential Intro To Frontiers of Bodywork

Did you know that muscles aren’t the only tissues that store tension - there’s also fascia, bone, nerve, and vascular tension? Or perhaps that gentle touch can induce oceanic, nondual states of consciousness, from within which - body tissues will spontaneously self-reorganize to heal aches, injuries, and postural distortions?

In this session, bodyworker Elena Lake will share her story of how her initial experience in math, physics, and machine learning at MIT and Meta convinced her that actually, Well-Trained Human Senses Are The Most Mathematically Rigorous Wellness Approach when it came to healing her severe chronic disease. She’ll be joined by Dr. Myles O’Donnell for a clinical demo of anatomically-diagnostic bodywork on audience volunteers, and audience members will get a chance to feel the major tension categories on one another and themselves.

text:
re: teaching people how to feel, this morning I’m musing on - a common beginner question “how do I know if what I’m feeling is real”

I think a lot of bodywork teachings err on the side of “what you’re feeling is right, trust it” but imo intuition can be wrong too. you find out if you’re onto something, by checking. and it calibrates over time - getting increasingly more accurate with increased reps. but it is possible to feel things that do NOT track the client’s experience, too. so the practice for learning is to make low-stakes predictions and have a repertoire of low-stakes checks. for example, try assigning your intuition the task of “where would this person like <xyz quality of touch>” that fits naturally into the flow of a massage without disrupting it. and see if they exhale or if their tissue softens (a sign that you were right) or if not (a sign that you were not). I do NOT recommend starting intuition training with something like “what is this person’s biggest trauma” because I cannot think of a smooth easy graceful polite nondisruptive way to check that. 

overall I really like massage as a training tool for kinesthetic intuition because the feedback cycles on kinesthetic intuition are immediate and cheap. not so for something like physical therapy, where - you assign them exercises and have to wait at least two weeks to see if they did them and if it helped.

text:
I just finished taking a 3day class about brain membranes and vasculature! 

my main takeaways:

* omg I LOVE having practice partners with high overlap and skill of perceptual space. for the first two days I was paired with someone high-palpatory sensitivity, and I explained the exact shape of the stiffness and sludginess behind my right eye and we both observed in wonder as systematically unsticking the brain dura from the skull, bone by bone, did, in fact, ripple out through the soreness - it would decrease by ~30% but crop up somewhere else. and he saw it too! and then we essentially chased it around my head (hello aftereffects from old injury) and I felt the steady decrease in the layers and it was lovely. 

super cool to have shared reality about “yeah it’s slightly pinched up behind my right eye -> oh yep the spot you’re touching is interacting with that, oh and now it moved over to that spot on my right parietal and you also feel that? cool!” 

* argh, order of operation really matters 

third day I was not so lucky. I was paired with someone who had more of an outside model of how the body “should” be, who unstuck my left temporal bone and I felt BAD. Insta-dissociated, my hearing felt muffled and balance felt off, I felt emotional like a raw live wire and had a big swell of sadness - couldn’t focus and had to hide in a side room to cry. 

I tell him this does not seem like what my body wanted and he says “well do you want your left temporal bone to be more stuck than the right?” and I just, gaped. Felt very validated in my own models of “just because it’s stuck doesn’t mean release it! follow the tissue reorganization response and the arrow of relaxation. don’t release things just because.”

* another order of operations win: feeling a particular restriction as “underneath” another 

I was sensing a distortion of his right occiput <-> dura but it straightforwardly felt like it was “within the bounding box” of a differently shaped restriction that originated around his right pec or lung area. 

* wow the teacher fixed my abdominal vasculature. there were areas of my abdomen that could only activate in stiff blocks all at once, even if I did, say, a spinal wave. he did some vascular work and the blockiness is just gone, now it’s smooth and light and flowy and I am using 15% less effort. also my digestion is chiller and less gassy; cool overlap with @jessicavellela. of course there are still layers of stiffness I can feel because I’m a professional at it, but this one session with him was roughly the movement-smoothing I’d expect from 3months of Ayurveda + 6hr/week athletic training (though albeit without the strength gains).
